script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Tuesday — here is your QQQM recap. The QQQ portfolio closed zero point seven six percent down on the session. A split market today — some names held firm, others got hit hard. Semiconductors were the pressure point, and one chip name in particular delivered the kind of single-day loss that demands a full explanation.

Samsung reported earnings, and the ripple hit chipmakers across the board. Concerns that the artificial intelligence rally may be running ahead of fundamentals sent investors to the exits in select semiconductor names. Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA Corporation, and Intel all fell more than seven percent in Tuesday's session. That is not noise. That is a rotation signal, and it landed squarely inside this portfolio.

The Nasdaq closed lower on Tuesday as semiconductor stocks led declines across the technology sector. Concerns about the pace of artificial intelligence-driven demand, compounded by Samsung's earnings report which stoked fears of oversupply and unmet expectations, weighed on chip-linked equities. Rising competition from Chinese manufacturers added additional pressure. The broader selloff in semiconductors dragged on the Nasdaq composite, which finished the session in negative territory, with the S&P five hundred also closing down as risk appetite softened across growth-oriented positions.

The lead story of Tuesday's session belongs to the semiconductor equipment complex — and it is directly tied to a single catalyst: Samsung's earnings report. According to coverage from the Economic Times, Samsung's results fanned fears that the artificial intelligence-driven demand cycle may be overstretched, triggering a broad reassessment of valuations across chip-linked names. Micron Technology bore the most visible damage among the primary holdings, falling three dollars and eighty-one cents in percentage terms — closing at nine hundred thirty-eight dollars and thirty-eight cents, down thirty-seven dollars and eighteen cents. But the deeper damage landed in the secondary holdings. Applied Materials dropped eight point zero five percent. KLA Corporation fell eight point one zero percent. Lam Research shed seven point one nine percent. A separate report from finance dot yahoo dot com cited research from SemiAnalysis indicating that Nvidia's next major AI rack-scale system — the Kyber NVL one forty-four — has been delayed from a planned twenty twenty-seven launch to twenty twenty-eight due to a printed circuit board manufacturing issue. Nvidia publicly disputed the report. Nevertheless, the headline circulated during the session and added to investor unease across the semiconductor space. For QQQM holders, the concentration in chip-related names means this sector's volatility carries outsized portfolio implications.

Turning to Nvidia — the largest holding in QQQM at seven point six percent of the portfolio. Nvidia closed at one hundred ninety-six dollars and ninety-three cents, up one point zero eight percent on the session. That is a notable result given the pressure across the semiconductor space. The gain came despite the circuit board delay report circulating around Nvidia's Kyber system. Nvidia disputed the SemiAnalysis findings, and the market appeared to give the company the benefit of the doubt. Separately, a Yahoo Finance piece flagged Nvidia among artificial intelligence stocks that Wall Street analysts believe could surge at least forty percent over the next twelve months. Also of note: SpaceX joined the Nasdaq one hundred index in Tuesday's session, a development that has structural implications for passive funds tracking the index — and by extension, for QQQM's composition going forward. Watch for any rebalancing commentary in the coming sessions.

Over at Apple, the stock closed at three hundred ten dollars and sixty-six cents, up zero point six six percent. Apple was a quiet positive on the day. Yahoo Finance coverage noted that Apple has underappreciated price elasticity of demand — meaning analysts believe the company can absorb hardware price increases without significant volume loss. That is a meaningful buffer in an environment where consumer spending faces pressure. Apple's relative outperformance against the broader Nasdaq pullback reinforces its defensive characteristics within the growth-oriented QQQM structure.

Alphabet closed at three hundred sixty-seven dollars and three cents, up one point nine eight percent — one of the stronger performers among the primary holdings on Tuesday. No Alphabet-specific fundamental news drove the move, but the stock benefited from rotation into names perceived as having diversified artificial intelligence exposure beyond pure semiconductor plays. Amazon's bond issuance — twenty-five billion dollars raised to fund artificial intelligence infrastructure — was cited in Yahoo Finance coverage that grouped Alphabet within the broader cloud and AI infrastructure narrative. Watch Alphabet's advertising revenue trends as the next major fundamental catalyst.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred thirty-eight dollars and thirty-eight cents, down three point eight one percent — the steepest loss among the primary holdings on Tuesday. As covered in the top story, Samsung's earnings triggered the decline. Yahoo Finance also noted that the Micron weighting in the Nasdaq one hundred — at five point seven five percent — significantly outweighs Meta Platforms at two point six six percent, despite Meta carrying a substantially larger market capitalization. That structural quirk means Micron's volatility punches above its intuitive weight in the index. A separate piece noted Micron's upbeat prior forecast as a potential positive for momentum-oriented funds, but Tuesday's price action suggests the near-term sentiment has shifted bearish.

Meanwhile, Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-eight dollars and eighty-four cents, down zero point four two percent. A modest decline on low news volume. Microsoft remains a core artificial intelligence infrastructure holding through its Azure cloud platform and its deep integration with OpenAI. The pullback is within normal session variance and does not reflect any fundamental development. The next meaningful catalyst for Microsoft is its fiscal year earnings report, which the market will watch closely for Azure growth metrics.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-five dollars and ninety-eight cents, up one point three six percent. The catalyst was clear: Amazon announced a twenty-five billion dollar bond offering across eight tranches to fund artificial intelligence infrastructure investment. Bloomberg and The Wall Street Journal both covered the filing. The scale of the capital raise signals aggressive commitment to AI-linked infrastructure spending — data centers, compute capacity, and related buildout. The market responded positively, treating the move as a confidence signal rather than a leverage concern.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred sixteen dollars and eleven cents, down zero point three three percent. A marginal decline with no specific news catalyst in Tuesday's session. Advanced Micro Devices has been trading in the shadow of Nvidia's dominance in the AI accelerator market, and the delay headlines around competing chip architectures are a mixed backdrop. Watch for any data center customer announcements as the next potential re-rating event.

Tesla closed at four hundred two dollars and ninety cents, up two point four zero percent — the strongest performer among the primary holdings on a percentage basis Tuesday. No Tesla-specific news drove the move in Tuesday's session. The gain appears to reflect broader rotation into consumer discretionary and electric vehicle names as semiconductor stocks sold off. Tesla's price action continues to be influenced by sentiment around Elon Musk and any developments tied to his other ventures, including SpaceX's Nasdaq one hundred inclusion.

Intel closed at one hundred ten dollars and thirty-nine cents, down eight point two eight percent — the worst single-session percentage decline among the primary holdings on Tuesday. No Intel-specific headline drove the drop in isolation, but the stock was caught directly in the Samsung-triggered semiconductor selloff. Intel's ongoing turnaround narrative — centered on its foundry business and competitive positioning against Taiwan Semiconductor — faces renewed skepticism when the broader chip sector comes under pressure. This is a name to watch closely over the next twenty-four hours for any analyst commentary in response to Tuesday's move.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any Nvidia response or clarification regarding the Kyber system delay report from SemiAnalysis — Nvidia disputed the findings, and a formal statement or investor day comment could move the stock materially. Second, watch for analyst reactions to Intel's eight point two eight percent single-session decline — price target revisions or ratings changes could arrive as early as Wednesday morning. Third, monitor Samsung's follow-through commentary and any additional semiconductor earnings from the Asian supply chain — these will set the tone for the chip complex heading into the back half of the week.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Keep an eye on the semiconductor equipment names — Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA Corporation all posted severe single-session losses, and whether those hold or reverse Wednesday will tell you a great deal about where this portfolio is headed.
